query: abdominal cramps vomiting watery diarrhea enterotoxigenic e coli

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Diarrhea -- History and Physical -- Enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli
retrieved_docs.content: ETEC is a common cause of epidemic diarrheal illnesses in resource-limited areas, as well as traveler's diarrhea, due to the ingestion of fecally contaminated food and water. Common symptoms are vomiting, stomach cramps, headache, and low-grade fever. Rapid dehydration can occur within a few hours.

retrieved_docs.title: Escherichia coli (e Coli 0157 H7)(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients will classically present with acute onset of bloody diarrhea and abdominal cramping, usually without fever. Diarrhea initially may not be bloody, often being watery in consistency. Most patients will not manifest a temperature during the initial presentation and evaluation. As a result of nausea, vomiting, and profuse diarrhea, patients will often note dehydration, asthenia, and decreased urine output. Patient history should include assessment of risk factors including (1) consumption of raw or undercooked meats or dairy products, especially unpasteurized milk, (2) exposure to ruminant animals or petting zoos, and (3) exposure to other farm scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterohemorrhagic Escherichia coli -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A history of exposure to contaminated sources, including food and drinking water, or close contact with ruminants, is often reported. EHEC clinically manifests as bloody or watery diarrhea without fever and typically a white blood cell count above 10,000/μL, sometimes associated with abdominal cramping. Diarrhea initially may not be bloody, often being watery in consistency. Most patients will not manifest a temperature during the initial presentation and evaluation. As a result of nausea, vomiting, and profuse diarrhea, patients will often note dehydration, asthenia, and decreased urine output. Systemic signs of dehydration such as dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, decreased skin turgor, slow capillary refill, cold extremities, and delirium, presage worsened morbidity, particularly in children. The incubation period between exposure to EHEC and the onset of symptoms is typically 3 to 4 days. [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Escherichia coli (e Coli 0157 H7)(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: First isolated in 1982, Shiga toxin-producing Escherichia coli O157: H7 has become an important food and waterborne pathogen that causes diarrhea, hemorrhagic colitis, and hemolytic-uremic syndrome (HUS) in humans. An enterohemorrhagic bacterial strain, E. coli O157: H7 infects the alimentary tract and induces abdominal cramps with hemorrhagic diarrhea. Transmission of E. coli O157: H7 occurs via the fecal-oral route after consumption of contaminated, undercooked liquids and foods. Alternatively, E. coli 0157: H7 can be transmitted by person-to-person through fecal shedding and accounts for an estimated 11% of infections. The production of Shiga toxins is a key factor contributing to the development of HUS. Enterohemorrhagic E. coli O157: H7 induces illness secondary to its production of Shiga toxin that causes a range of gastrointestinal illnesses, from watery diarrhea to hemorrhagic colitis. E. coli 0157: H7 induces enterohemorrhagic disease that can cause systemic illness by hemolytic uremic syndrome, which manifests as hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute renal failure. HUS can result in both acute, potentially life-threatening illness and lifelong, chronic illness. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.content: Enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli : Fecal contamination of food or water and travel to resource-limited areas

retrieved_docs.title: Escherichia coli (e Coli 0157 H7)(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal tenderness on physical examination reflects E. coli 0157: H7 Shiga toxin-induced intestinal vasculitis and inflammation. The abdominal discomfort from enterohemorrhagic E. coli generally manifests more severely than other forms of enteritis as a result of the hemorrhagic vasculitis. Systemic signs of dehydration such as dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, decreased skin turgor, slow capillary refill, cold extremities, and delirium, presage worsened morbidity, particularly in children.

retrieved_docs.title: Escherichia coli Infection -- Treatment / Management -- Bloody Diarrhea
retrieved_docs.content: Rehydration Oral fluids, if tolerated IV fluids if oral fluids not tolerated or clinically indicated Antimotility agents Not recommended for patients with dysentery as these may prolong illness Antibiotics Not recommended for patients with presumed EHEC/STEC Adults Fluoroquinolones Ciprofloxacin: 750 mg once; or 500 mg BID for three days Levofloxacin: 500 mg once; or 500 mg daily for three days Azithromycin: 1,000 mg once; or 500 mg daily for three days Rifaximin: 400 mg BID or 200 mg TID for three days Children Azithromycin: 10 mg/kg on day 1, 5 mg/kg on days 2 and 3

retrieved_docs.title: Enterohemorrhagic Escherichia coli -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Serotype O157:H7 is the most well-recognized E coli strain  responsible for global outbreaks of bloody diarrhea and HUS. The condition arises from the development of Shiga-like toxins that disrupt the intestinal cell membranes. This disruption leads to the loss of water and electrolytes, as well as the infiltration of inflammatory cells, which causes further cell injury. Complications include gastrointestinal and acute kidney injury, including HUS.

retrieved_docs.title: Diarrhea -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Common complications of common pathogens are: Aeromonas caviae - Intussusception, hemolytic-uremic syndrome (HUS), gram-negative sepsis Campylobacter species - Bacteremia, meningitis, urinary tract infection, pancreatitis, cholecystitis, Reiter syndrome (RS) C difficile - Chronic diarrhea C perfringens - Enteritis necroticans Plesiomonas species - Septicemia Enterohemorrhagic E coli O157:H7 - HUS Enterohemorrhagic E coli - Hemorrhagic colitis Salmonella species - Seizures, RS, HUS, perforation, enteric fever Vibrio species - Rapid dehydration Giardia species - Chronic fat malabsorption Rotavirus - Isotonic dehydration, carbohydrate intolerance Y enterocolitica - Appendicitis, intussusception, perforation, toxic megacolon, peritonitis, cholangitis, bacteremia, RS Cryptosporidium species - Chronic diarrhea Entamoeba species - Liver abscess, colonic perforation

retrieved_docs.title: Infectious Colitis(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Enterotoxigenic E coli is the leading cause of traveler’s diarrhea. Enteroaggregative E coli can cause traveler’s diarrhea, but it is not the leading cause. Enterohemorrhagic E coli has 2 main serotypes, E.coli O157:H7, and non-O157:H7; the natural reservoirs of both serotypes are cows. Therefore, the infection is related to consuming inadequately cooked beef or contaminated milk or vegetables. They are responsible for outbreaks in developed countries. [1] Two of these subgroups cause nonbloody diarrhea: enterotoxigenic E coli and enteroaggregative E. coli ; both produce enterotoxins that induce chloride and water secretion and inhibit their absorption. However, enterohemorrhagic E coli (both strains E coli O157:H7 and non-O157:H7) causes bloody diarrhea and produces Shiga-like toxins, resulting in a clinical picture similar to Shigella dysenteriae infection. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacillus Cereus -- History and Physical -- Gastrointestinal Syndromes
retrieved_docs.content: For diarrheal-type illnesses, symptoms include profuse watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, and cramping. Nausea and vomiting are seldom seen. Symptom onset is usually within 6 to 15 hours of eating food left at room temperature for more than 2 hours. The toxin associated with this form of the illness is produced in the small intestine of the patient after ingestion of the bacilli or the spores. Pore-forming cytotoxins (hemolysin BL, nonhemolytic enterotoxin, and cytotoxin K) are secreted by the ingested vegetative cells. These toxins are heat-labile.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Diarrhea -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Acute diarrheal illnesses pose a serious global public health threat, and prompt and effective treatment is crucial. The World Health Organization characterizes diarrhea as the passage of loose or watery stools 3 or more times a day. Viral infections cause most acute diarrhea and typically resolve with supportive care. Bacterial infections can present with symptoms that may overlap with those of viral gastroenteritis. Key clinical features suggesting a bacterial etiology include fever, bloody or mucoid stools, severe abdominal pain, recent antibiotic use, and hospitalization. Common bacterial pathogens include Salmonella , Shigella , Campylobacter jejuni , Yersinia enterocolitica , Clostridioides difficile , and Escherichia coli . While most bacterial infections are self-limited, they can lead to severe complications like sepsis, seizures, hemolytic uremic syndrome, Guillain-Barré syndrome, and death.

retrieved_docs.title: Diarrhea -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Travel history is important as it may direct towards the underlying causative agent of infectious diarrhea. Enterotoxigenic E coli is by far the leading cause of traveler's diarrhea. Following are some common associations between certain areas and causative pathogens:

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Diarrhea -- History and Physical -- Enterohemorrhagic Escherichia coli
retrieved_docs.content: STEC are strains of Escherichia coli that contain genes encoding Shiga toxin 1 or 2. Ingestion of contaminated food and water, animal contact, and person-to-person spread are all potential modes of transmission for STEC. Strains with genes that produce Shiga toxin 2 have a high propensity for causing HUS. The average incubation period is 3 days, ranging from 1 to 10. The initial symptoms are painful, nonbloody diarrhea that becomes bloody after 1 to 3 days. Fever is an uncommon finding and, if present, should raise the suspicion for alternative causes like Shigella , Campylobacter , or Salmonella . The diarrhea associated with STEC typically resolves after 7 days.

retrieved_docs.title: Entamoeba coli Infection -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Patients may rarely show gastrointestinal symptoms such as loose stools, colicky abdominal pain, and flatulence.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Diarrhea -- History and Physical -- Clostridium perfringens
retrieved_docs.content: Clostridium perfringens causes foodborne, toxin-mediated, watery diarrhea second only to Salmonella species in resource-rich settings. Like Bacillus species, the spores from type A strains can survive at standard cooking temperatures and thrive in improperly stored foods. Common sources are incorrectly reheated meats, gravies, and poultry. Watery diarrhea and abdominal cramping are the primary symptoms occurring within 6 to 24 hours, while fever and vomiting are uncommon. Symptoms generally resolve within 24 to 48 hours. Type C strains produce a β-toxin that results in pigbel or hemorrhagic necrosis of the jejunum, also known as enteritis necroticans, most often encountered after pork consumption in resource-limited countries.

retrieved_docs.content: Constipation (إِمْساك) is clinically associated with vomiting (تَقَيُّء), indicating that patients who experience difficulty passing stool may also present with episodes of emesis, reflecting a possible shared gastrointestinal dysfunction or underlying condition that links these two symptoms.

retrieved_docs.content: Alosetron is a pharmacologic agent that may be employed in the treatment of diarrhea (الإِسْهال), indicating its therapeutic role in managing this gastrointestinal condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1915
retrieved_docs.content: Vomiting alone has a relationship with antiemetika, highlighting that antiemetic medications are indicated for the management of isolated vomiting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2299
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are both used therapeutically to manage diarrhea (الإِسْهال) and broader gastrointestinal disturbances (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ). In contrast, magnesium hydroxide (hydroxid hořečnatý) can provoke diarrhea as an adverse effect. Thus, while magnesium hydroxide may induce diarrheal symptoms, the bismuth compounds serve as treatments for those same symptoms and for related gastrointestinal disorders, highlighting their complementary roles in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1403
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate is recognized as a therapeutic agent that may be used to treat الإِسْهال (diarrhea), whereas hydroxid hořečnatý (magnesium hydroxide) can induce الإِسْهال, reflecting its well‑known laxative effect. Understanding that bismuth subnitrate can help alleviate diarrheal symptoms while magnesium hydroxide may precipitate them is important for clinicians when selecting appropriate treatments and managing potential side effects in patients with gastrointestinal disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: Other mental conditions are classified as القيء نفسي المنشأ, a term that denotes psychogenic vomiting—a condition in which vomiting arises from psychological factors rather than an organic physiological cause. This classification highlights the importance of recognizing psychological origins when evaluating vomiting symptoms, ensuring appropriate mental‑health assessment and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1759
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are both therapeutic agents that may be used to treat الإِسْهال (diarrhea) and اضطراب معدي (gastrointestinal disorder). Each of these bismuth compounds shares the same indicated uses, suggesting they have comparable efficacy in managing diarrheal conditions and related gastrointestinal disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1797
retrieved_docs.content: Bismuth subnitrate and bismuth subcitrate are both therapeutic agents that may be used to treat gastrointestinal disorders (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ) as well as diarrhea (الإِسْهال). In addition, alosetron is recognized as a medication that can be employed in the management of diarrhea. Together, these drugs provide clinicians with multiple options for addressing diarrheal symptoms and broader intestinal disturbances, supporting comprehensive care for patients with gastrointestinal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2785
retrieved_docs.content: Alosetron and bismuth subcitrate are medications that may be used to treat الإِسْهال (diarrhea). Alosetron, a selective 5‑HT3 receptor antagonist, is indicated for severe diarrhea‑predominant irritable bowel syndrome, while bismuth subcitrate, a bismuth‑based compound, possesses antidiarrheal and gastroprotective properties. Both agents represent pharmacologic options for managing الإِسْهال, underscoring the importance of targeted drug therapy in the treatment of diarrheal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1900
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium citrate may be used to treat إِمْساك (constipation), and إِمْساك is clinically associated with تَقَيُّء (vomiting). This relationship indicates that alleviating constipation with magnesium citrate could also impact the occurrence or severity of vomiting, highlighting the interconnected nature of gastrointestinal symptoms and the therapeutic role of magnesium citrate in managing constipation‑related clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 373
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium citrate is an osmotic laxative that may be used to treat إِمْساك, but its administration is contraindicated in patients with ثقب معوي because it can worsen a perforated intestine. إِمْساك is also clinically associated with تَقَيُّء, indicating that constipation and diarrhea often occur together in functional bowel disorders. When considering magnesium citrate for symptom relief, clinicians must ensure the absence of ثقب معوي and be aware of the potential shift from إِمْساك to تَقَيُّء, tailoring treatment to maintain safe and effective gastrointestinal management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1567
retrieved_docs.content: CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES are closely linked to formulations that combine electrolytes with carbohydrates and may also incorporate antidiarrheals as well as intestinal anti‑inflammatory/anti‑infective agents, reflecting their role in restoring fluid and electrolyte balance while simultaneously treating diarrheal disease and intestinal inflammation or infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2226
retrieved_docs.content: Enema is classified as a gastrointestinal preparation, used to cleanse the bowel and facilitate evacuation of intestinal contents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2558
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs for functional gastrointestinal disorders are classified as propulsives, a group of medications that enhance gastrointestinal motility and are used to manage functional GI conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1488
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely linked to, and may be considered synonymous with, the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. Within this category, the specific manifestation of abdominal pain (ألم بطني) is identified as a child symptom, illustrating how particular clinical presentations fall under the umbrella of ill‑defined condition signs and symptoms. This relationship underscores the importance of recognizing individual symptoms such as abdominal pain as part of a broader, sometimes nonspecific, clinical picture.
